Good morning,

A quick bit of housekeeping:

* First, I missed this tweet from Elon Musk ( https://twitter.com/elonmusk/=
status/1640800519894736896 ) that clarified that the =E2=80=98For You=E2=80=
=99 tab will also include accounts you follow; that was not clear to me, al=

 talk to smart people here. But the way I arrive at that is, number one, ju=
st take GPT-4. We know how to make it, but we don=E2=80=99t really know how=
 it works. There=E2=80=99s no one on Earth that really knows, truly, what=
=E2=80=99s going on inside of that thing. It=E2=80=99s probably arguably th=
e most complex artifact we=E2=80=99ve ever created in our civilization. It=
=E2=80=99s got over a trillion parameters. Just inferencing it, I=E2=80=99m=
 guessing, takes, I don=E2=80=99t know, sixteen or twenty-four A100s. A sin=
gle A100 can do something like 300 trillion floating point operations per s=
econd, so if you=E2=80=99ve got sixteen of them, you=E2=80=99re doing, I do=
n=E2=80=99t know what that is, four or five quadrillion floating point oper=
ations per second. That=E2=80=99s a lot, that=E2=80=99s crazy. So they=E2=
=80=99re kind of these big blobs of math and we don=E2=80=99t actually know=
 what=E2=80=99s going on. Literally, I=E2=80=99ve tried to find the person =
who knows what=E2=80=99s happening inside of there, we can explain it at th=
e quantum mechanical level, but the phenomenology above that, no one really=
 understands.

Then, I=E2=80=99ve had access to it for months but people are still showing=
 me things, every day, on Twitter and elsewhere that it can do that I didn=
=E2=80=99t realize. I=E2=80=99d played with it, but I hadn=E2=80=99t found =
them. So there=E2=80=99s hidden capabilities in there that we don=E2=80=99t=
 know about. It=E2=80=99s big, it=E2=80=99s complicated, we don=E2=80=99t k=
now how it works, it=E2=80=99s got this capability overhang built into it. =
Then there are clear examples where we=E2=80=99re still learning to control=
 it, where it=E2=80=99s going to go off and do something we don=E2=80=99t w=
ant it to do. I think the Bing Chat Sydney example was a very public demons=
tration of how even Microsoft, who=E2=80=99s very closely partnered with Op=
enAI and is full of smart researchers, they can even have one of these thin=
gs slip the leash and go off and do things it=E2=80=99s not supposed to. Th=
en I=E2=80=99d just say, we=E2=80=99re really eager to hook these things up=
, we talked about the Wolverine.py example, or just running code without re=
ally reading it, I think that is going to be normal.

*Whenever I got access to plug-ins, the access web plug-in was gone, I=E2=
=80=99m not sure what happened there, but I can imagine =E2=80=94 it didn=
=E2=80=99t seem to exist for long.*

*NF:* Somebody said there=E2=80=99s a way in which the plug-ins were like s=
eeing the humanoid robot power on, and this frisson of energy just passed t=
hrough the body and it sits up on the table or whatever. It=E2=80=99s not j=
ust talking to you, it=E2=80=99s now taking action in the world on your beh=
alf, or God knows exactly what it=E2=80=99s doing and that was sort of a mo=
ment.

Then the other thing that I=E2=80=99ve started to think about is, maybe hum=
an intelligence is not so impressive. Maybe our intelligence, which has see=
med so singular and unique, maybe it just can be exceeded. This is not a th=
eoretical thing or a fun sci-fi idea, but maybe it=E2=80=99s just something=
 very practically we can do, and very, very quickly. You take GPT-4, you ad=
d a little scratch pad, you add some memory, you throw it into a loop, you =
give it the Internet and a bank account. Like, what=E2=80=99s going to happ=
en? It=E2=80=99s sort of hard to predict exactly.

I think then my final thought is that over time, these things will not just=
 respond to queries but they=E2=80=99ll be agents, they=E2=80=99ll execute =
plans. People are already in the sort of hustle GPT corner of the internet,=
 empowering them to run e-commerce businesses autonomously to see what happ=
ens. So there are these agents that are copyable, and they=E2=80=99re also =
subject to some kind of selection pressure. Just where does that go from a =
Darwinian point of view? I don=E2=80=99t know, I=E2=80=99m still sort of th=
inking this through.

But I think these are really legitimate, they sound crazy, but they=E2=80=
=99re legitimate concerns and I think there=E2=80=99s a lot of people, outs=
ide of the tech folks, who are just naturally creeped out by AI. I think wh=
atever that instinct is, it has something in it that=E2=80=99s essentially =
worth paying attention to, and those of us who are just purely boosters, wh=
ich I have certainly been and probably still am largely, should think about=
 it a little bit.

*Number one, I don=E2=80=99t know. I think that is sort of the big picture =
takeaway for literally all of this, is we are in completely unprecedented, =
uncharted territory and I think part of the reaction against AI existential=
ism is some of the people that talk about AI risk are so absolutist in thei=
r statement that there=E2=80=99s a natural pushback against that =E2=80=94*

*NF:* Yeah, definitely.

*=E2=80=94you don=E2=80=99t want to grant any sort of room there. But at th=
e same time, you mention the selection pressure. We=E2=80=99ve talked about=
 the open source models, there was a model that came out in China that is n=
ot as capable ( https://twitter.com/Baidu_Inc/status/1622856436295376902?s=
=3D20 ) , but it exists and if we know anything in tech over the last forty=
 years, it=E2=80=99s that the level of capability on the day something is l=
aunched is much less important than whether or not it exists. The fact that=
 LLaMA runs on a Raspberry Pi is meaningful, not because you=E2=80=99re goi=
ng to use it on a Raspberry Pi, it=E2=80=99s because it shows that, inevita=
bly it=E2=80=99s out there, and it=E2=80=99s going to happen and that=E2=80=
=99s the reality of this stuff. It=E2=80=99s all a matter of timing at this=
 point.*

*There=E2=80=99s a bit about the Internet, like you can talk about the Inte=
rnet, was this actually good for society? It doesn=E2=80=99t really matter.=
 That=E2=80=99s a nice discussion to have around a fire late at night or wh=
atever your recreational choice is, because it is here and the only way to =
figure out these issues is to go forward. It feels like we=E2=80=99ve alrea=
dy crossed that Rubicon with this AI stuff, so let=E2=80=99s push forward a=
nd figure it out and if we blow ourselves up in process, well, it was proba=
bly going to happen regardless.*

*NF:* It definitely does seem like there=E2=80=99s just unlimited enthusias=
m, and that=E2=80=99s probably where we=E2=80=99re headed. But yeah, I mean=
, I think it=E2=80=99s an interesting question and no longer theoretical an=
d no longer fictional.

*To your point, the GPT-4 was built on A100s, chips that are out there and =
exist, right? And to your point, GPT-4 has so much capability that it=E2=80=
=99s going to take us years to digest it and figure out what can be done wi=
th it. Even if you think the door to the barn should be shut, the horse is =
over the horizon, it=E2=80=99s a moot point at this point.*

*NF:* Yeah, I think it=E2=80=99s interesting. I do think there will probabl=
y be a market for tools to better =E2=80=94 we have dog training businesses=
, to Daniel=E2=80=99s pet point. You can send your dog off to finishing sch=
ool to get trained or there=E2=80=99s techniques for teaching your pet to b=
e house-trained, or even having guard dogs that will attack intruders, but =
not you. So, yeah, I think there=E2=80=99s going to be a real market need f=
or tools and techniques just to do quality assurance on these things, valid=
ate them, ensure they do the things you want and not the things you don=E2=
=80=99t want. Probably the more powerful they are, and the more we hook the=
m up, the greater the demand for those techniques will be, and it would be =
cool if in some areas we got ahead there and didn=E2=80=99t merely react. A=
lthough we=E2=80=99ll probably mostly react.

*Well, the other issue is the fact it=E2=80=99s language is super important=
 and meaningful, because I think the vector of risk is much less the Termin=
ator, it figures out how to operate machines and do X, Y, Z. It=E2=80=99s g=
oing to be it convincing people to do things. The brain-blood barrier sort =
of has already been broken in that regard, because language is the ultimate=
 mode of virality, it=E2=80=99s already there.*

*DG:* One of the reasons I keep on harping about the output of the models r=
eally mattering now, is we=E2=80=99ve had these systems for creating tokens=
 that drive humans insane for ten years now, and it=E2=80=99s called social=
 media, and it does drive people insane. The nice thing about the system th=
at we have now is we have the opportunity to control the types of tokens it=
 emits to people in a much more specific way, I think, than we did with soc=
ial media, where you would just get random tweets and retweets and you can=
=E2=80=99t really shadow ban people because they find out they=E2=80=99ve b=
een shadow banned. I do think we have a choice, whether these models drive =
people to be more open and happy or whether they drive people insane, based=
 on what they say.

I agree with you that the main risk is not that they shut down the Azure da=
ta center, but just that they start driving people to do wild things. I do =
sort of think if you could force all AIs to just be funnier, from some drea=
m regulation where that actually would work, which is impossible. But in th=
at hypothetical world, everything would be better. Because of the centraliz=
ation dynamic with these language models, the tokens are ultimately outputt=
ed by one of five companies now. I think you can actually do much better th=
an the current status quo, which is social media emitting tokens that reall=
y drive people up the wall, and have obviously have hurt our country quite =
a bit and the world as well, just in terms of balkanizing everyone.

*It=E2=80=99s a phenomenal point, because I think there=E2=80=99s a good ch=
ance we look at this twenty-year run as a total aberration, that on one han=
d was useful to give these models the sort of raw language that they needed=
 to learn. But was actually, all things considered, a pretty crappy experie=
nce for everyone.*

*DG:* Right?

*I mentioned this on a recent Sharp Tech episode ( https://sharptech.fm/mem=
ber/episode/evaluating-ai-risk-and-entering-an-era-of-incredible-unknowns )=
 , but I feel like I=E2=80=99m living in the future, in this regard specifi=
cally, where basically all of my social interaction is in encrypted chat ap=
ps. It=E2=80=99s in a high trust environment with people that I like hangin=
g out with, and the way things can be perceived and whatever is just so muc=
h better, and it=E2=80=99s a genuine, meaningful improvement in my life. Th=
is idea of public social media was absolutely insane. Like, how can AI be w=
orse than this idea that you=E2=80=99re going to just put your stuff out th=
ere on the Internet? Anyone can drive by and take shots at you or criticize=
 you, or you can become the current thing of the day. It=E2=80=99s just an =
all-around bad idea. To your point, Daniel, it=E2=80=99s just going to be m=
ore pleasant to interact with this AI, and people view that as a bad thing,=
 but I think that=E2=80=99s just an inherent distrust of the future and imp=
roperly evaluating where we=E2=80=99re at right now. Every time I say that =
I think Twitter is a real big problem people get upset and they=E2=80=99re =
like, =E2=80=9COh, you=E2=80=99re so anti-Twitter.=E2=80=9D I=E2=80=99m lik=
e, =E2=80=9CNo. It=E2=80=99s not good! Humans were not meant to interface w=
ith the entire world in real time.=E2=80=9D*

*NF:* We are encountering, by the way, more and more companies that are rea=
lly interesting, that are, in a way we=E2=80=99ve kind of come to believe, =
competing with social media, with AI. You can think about these parasocial =
relationships that people have set up on social media, where you follow, I =
don=E2=80=99t know, The Rock on Instagram and The Rock writes a post and hu=
ndreds of thousands of people reply, and The Rock reads approximately zero =
of those replies and responds to even fewer. So what is that relationship t=
hat you have with The Rock? It=E2=80=99s not a real relationship. Then if y=
ou have an AI friend, online, that you can talk to, whether it=E2=80=99s Ch=
atGPT or character.ai.

*Bring back Sydney!*

*NF:* Yeah, or Sydney, whoever it is that gets you excited, then at least y=
ou know they=E2=80=99re going to read and reply, at least you have a two-wa=
y relationship. The numbers on these, even just pure entertainment, non-pro=
ductivity chatbots like Replika, or Character.ai, are crazy. People are spe=
nding hours a day.

*DG:* And they=E2=80=99re not even funny, just imagine how good they would =
be if they were funny. I=E2=80=99m serious! There was one effort we know of=
 that, the way they set it up is particularly convenient and I find myself =
reaching for it. And I say this as a person who has the great pleasure in b=
eing able to WhatsApp Nat Friedman and Ben Thompson at any time of the day,=
 it=E2=80=99s helpful to talk sometimes to these other things. I think it=
=E2=80=99s one of those ideas that sounds really out there to people. In my=
 view, it=E2=80=99s better, to Nat=E2=80=99s point, than the current status=
 quo, which are these parasocial relationships that drive loneliness, becau=
se it is like interacting with a human that never responds or has eye conta=
ct with you. That=E2=80=99s what responding and not getting a response back=
 feels like, I think, at a deep, basic level. That=E2=80=99s why people are=
 walking around, dazed, confused and depressed. Whereas these agents, espec=
ially if they=E2=80=99re made well, especially if they=E2=80=99re funny and=
 enlightening and whimsical, I think, will be much better.

To your point, Ben, we may treat the last two decades as a giant training r=
un in order to create this era of sort of infinite companionship, which hop=
efully is good. Now, look, it can get just as dystopian as it is good. Fire=
 can be used for arson, every technology has two sides. But this is where I=
 think there=E2=80=99s some element of responsibility, and everyone making =
these models to make them funny. I think that really, really matters.

*It=E2=80=99s a really good point. Nat, to take this full circle, six month=
s ago, you were like, =E2=80=9CWhere are the people that are making the pro=
ducts? There=E2=80=99s just a lot of research.=E2=80=9D Well, we answered t=
hat, but I do think there=E2=80=99s almost like the bizarro Eliezer sort of=
 optimist angle, which is actually, this is good and it=E2=80=99s not good =
because it=E2=80=99s more productive, it=E2=80=99s good because this is goi=
ng to make people happier. Again, the truth is probably going to be somewhe=
re in the middle, it=E2=80=99s not going to be one extreme one way or the o=
ther. But I see no reason why, if you think about all the expected value ou=
tcomes, yes, I grant there is this very dark outcome where the machines are=
 just so much smarter and somehow they gain volition, despite the fact it=
=E2=80=99s not clear how that bridge is going to be crossed. But it=E2=80=
=99s just as reasonable to have not utopian, but a very, relative to the te=
rrible 2010s view that actually, people are happier, they=E2=80=99re more p=
roductive, they actually have better real world relationships because this =
distinction between online and offline is actually more meaningful.*

*DG:* That=E2=80=99s right.

*This is the bit about me giving them more shit, right? It=E2=80=99s almost=
 a good thing to make online and digital separate from the real. The more d=
istinction the better, because that is going to incentivize you to invest m=
ore in the real world, which makes people happier. So I think this is a val=
id case to make.*

*NF:* There=E2=80=99s definitely upside scenarios here, which are extreme. =
And I recently re-watched that movie Her.

*I did too, yeah.*

*NF:* I think Daniel and I have talked about this. Did you watch it?

*Yeah. I rewatched it after the Sydney experience.*

*NF:* Yeah. I=E2=80=99d remembered it as this sort of dystopian kind of wor=
ld and there were certainly parts of it that still felt that way, but I saw=
 a different story when I watched it this time, which was =E2=80=A6

*He was happy!*

*NF:* Yeah! There was this AI robot that very patiently performed therapy o=
n this traumatized man and then released him at the moment he was ready to =
kind of re-enter the world of human relationships. It=E2=80=99s an incredib=
le story when you view it that way. He was greatly improved by his interact=
ions with this AI. I mean, hopefully that=E2=80=99s what we=E2=80=99re buil=
ding, I think probably we=E2=80=99re building that right now, to a much gre=
ater degree than most people appreciate.

*That=E2=80=99s a perfect note to end it on. Good to check in. I think we m=
ade it through most of the list; we didn=E2=80=99t get to Nvidia, but that=
=E2=80=99s fine. We can leave that for a few months from now. Nat, Daniel, =
it=E2=80=99s great to have you. And I=E2=80=99m sure we=E2=80=99ll check in=
 again soon.*
